articles.title: Scientists Create 'Optical Tornadoes' That Could Revolutionize Quantum Tech
articles.summary: Researchers have created swirling 'optical tornadoes' inside a tiny structure using liquid crystals. These stable light vortices, produced in the ground state for the first time, could simplify photonic devices for quantum communication and optical computing. The team from the University of Warsaw and collaborators used torons—spiral defects in liquid crystals—to trap light and make it spin. This breakthrough could lead to smaller, more scalable light sources.

articles.title: Massive Lava Tube Discovered on Venus—Bigger Than Any on Earth
articles.summary: By reanalyzing 1990s radar data from NASA's Magellan probe, scientists have discovered the first lava tube on Venus. Located near the volcano Nyx Mons, the tube is at least 375 meters deep and could be up to 1 kilometer wide—larger than any known lava tube on Earth or Mars. The finding, published in Nature Communications, raises questions about how such a large tube formed under Venus's Earth-like gravity. It also highlights the lasting value of old data and sets the stage for upcoming missions like VERITAS and EnVision.

articles.title: This Teacher Built a Life-Size ENIAC Replica With His Students—And It's Awesome
articles.summary: Tom Burick, a technology instructor at a school for neurodivergent students in Arizona, led his class in constructing a full-scale replica of ENIAC, one of the world's first programmable electronic computers. The project marks the 80th anniversary of ENIAC's construction. Burick, a former robotics entrepreneur who has dyscalculia, designed the project to leverage his students' strengths like hyperfocus and precision. The replica matches the original computer's appearance before it was disassembled in the 1950s.
